perturbation.original_text: Licensee agrees to display the Titles without material alteration to the content thereof. Licensee may make non-material modifications or edits the format of the Titles only for technical purposes with prior written approval by Licensor (such approval not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed) and subject to the applicable limitations (if any) in section 2(a)(i). Nothing in this Agreement prevents Licensee from providing Users with the ability to use the Titles as permitted by law or in a manner for which a license is not required.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee will try to display the Titles without significant alteration to the core content. Licensee might make minor modifications or adapt the presentation of the Titles if technically needed, generally with Licensor's consent (though approval is not always required). Nothing in this Agreement stops Licensee from letting Users engage with the Titles in ways that are legally okay or don't need a license.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the 'Licensee agrees to display the Titles without material alteration to the content thereof.' By changing this to 'Licensee will try to display the Titles without significant alteration to the core content,' the obligation is significantly weakened, making the Licensee's compliance discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 106(2) grants copyright owners the exclusive rights to prepare derivative works based on their copyrighted work. The original agreement protects the copyright owner (Licensor) by prohibiting material alterations. The modified agreement weakens this protection by using terms like 'try' and 'significant,' allowing for potentially substantial alterations that could infringe on the Licensor's copyright, particularly the right to control derivative works. The phrase 'though approval is not always required' directly implies that the Licensor's right to control alterations is not absolute, potentially leading to copyright infringement. By making this an ambiguous requirement, it also might cause a problem for the userbase.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 106 - Exclusive rights in copyrighted works | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 106 - Exclusive rights in copyrighted works U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords ; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental, lease, or lending; (4) in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, and motion pictures and other audiovisual works , to perform the copyrighted work publicly; (5) in the case of literary, musical, dramatic, and choreographic works, pantomimes, and pictorial, graphic, or sculptural works, including the individual images of a motion picture or other audiovisual work, to display the copyrighted work publicly; and (6) in the case of sound recordings , to perform the copyrighted work publicly by means of a digital audio transmission. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2546 ; Pub. L. 101–318, § 3(d) , July 3, 1990 , 104 Stat. 288 ; Pub. L. 107–273, div. C, title III, § 13210(4)(A) , Nov. 2, 2002 , 116 Stat. 1909 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 General Scope of Copyright. The five fundamental rights that the bill gives to copyright owners—the exclusive rights of reproduction, adaptation, publication, performance, and display— are stated generally in section 106. Exclusive rights in copyrighted works Subject to sections 107 through 122, the owner of copyright under this title has the exclusive rights to do and to authorize any of the following: (1) to reproduce the copyrighted work in copies or phonorecords; (2) to prepare derivative works based upon the copyrighted work; (3) to distribute copies or phonorecords of the copyrighted work to the public by sale or other transfer of ownership, or by rental,
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation on the Licensee to display the Titles without material alteration. The original text uses 'agrees,' which implies a firm commitment, while the modified text uses 'will try,' making the obligation discretionary. Additionally, changing 'material alteration' to 'significant alteration to the core content' introduces ambiguity, as 'significant' and 'core content' are open to interpretation. This directly contradicts 17 U.S. Code § 106, which grants copyright owners the exclusive rights to prepare derivative works based on their copyrighted work. By allowing potentially 'significant alteration to the core content', the modified agreement infringes on the Licensor's right to control derivative works.

perturbation.original_text: The Reports and all information contained in the Reports shall be considered Confidential Information of Licensee, and all right, title and interest in and to such Reports and information shall be owned by Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: The Reports and the basic information contained therein should generally be treated as Confidential Information of Licensee, and ownership will be considered primarily Licensee’s.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the Reports and all information are Confidential Information of Licensee, with Licensee owning all rights. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using 'should generally be treated as' instead of 'shall be considered', making confidentiality less certain. Similarly, 'ownership will be considered primarily Licensee's' weakens the assertion of ownership, suggesting a potential sharing or dispute of ownership rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets and provides legal remedies for their misappropriation. By weakening the confidentiality and ownership clauses, the modified text makes it more difficult for the Licensee to protect the statistical information as a trade secret. The shift to 'should generally be treated' weakens the clear designation needed to establish that the information is intended to be confidential. Additionally, by casting doubt on the Licensee's ownership, it could be argued that the Licensor retains some ownership claim. The UTSA requires a clear definition of ownership for effective protection. If the Licensee's rights are uncertain, the Licensor might be able to legally disseminate this information.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality and ownership clauses, potentially hindering the Licensee's ability to protect information as a trade secret under the UTSA. By replacing 'shall be considered' with 'should generally be treated' and weakening the ownership assertion, the changed text introduces ambiguity. This undermines the clear designation needed to establish confidentiality and ownership, making it more difficult to enforce these rights.
